content.text: SAST/DAST for Claude Application Codebases

Claude applications introduce security risks beyond standard web applications. User-supplied text flows into prompts (prompt injection risk), model outputs flow into downstream systems (output injection risk), and API keys must be kept out of code and logs. Static Application Security Testing (SAST) catches these issues in source code before deployment; Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST) validates the running application for vulnerabilities that only appear at runtime.

attrs.alt: SAST/DAST security testing pipeline for Claude applications

content.text: Claude-Specific Security Risks
Standard SAST tools catch generic vulnerabilities but miss patterns specific to LLM applications. These require custom rules.
Risk
Attack Vector
Detection Method
Prompt injection
User input appended directly to system prompt
SAST: detect f-string concatenation with user input into messages
API key hardcoding
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in source or test files
SAST: detect-secrets + custom Semgrep rule
Output reflection XSS
LLM output rendered as HTML without sanitization
DAST: inject HTML in LLM response, check if rendered
SSRF via tool calls
Tool execution fetches user-controlled URLs
SAST: detect urllib/requests with unvalidated url param
Sensitive data in logs
API responses logged including user PII
SAST: detect logging of anthropic response objects
Unconstrained shell exec
Tool calls subprocess with model-generated args
SAST: detect subprocess.run with variables from LLM
SAST Setup with Bandit and Semgrep
pip install bandit semgrep detect-secrets pip-audit
Bandit — Python Security Linter

# Run Bandit on the application code (not tests)
bandit -r app/ -ll -f json -o /tmp/bandit_report.json

# -ll: only report medium and high severity
# -f json: machine-readable output for CI parsing
# Common findings in Claude apps:
#   B105: hardcoded password / API key string
#   B603: subprocess call — common in tool executors
#   B108: probable insecure usage of temp file

# .bandit  — project-level config
[bandit]
skips = B101,B311
# B101: assert statements (acceptable in test code)
# B311: random — acceptable for non-security randomness
exclude_dirs = tests,docs
Custom Semgrep Rules for Claude Applications
Semgrep rules are YAML patterns that match AST-level code structures. Write rules for the Claude-specific risk patterns that generic scanners miss.

# security/semgrep-claude-rules.yaml
rules:
  - id: prompt-injection-f-string
    message: |
      User input interpolated directly into a prompt string.
      Sanitize or validate user_input before including it in messages.
    severity: ERROR
    languages: [python]
    patterns:
      - pattern: |
          anthropic.Anthropic().messages.create(
            ...,
            messages=[..., {"role": "user", "content": f"...$USER_INPUT..."}],
            ...
          )
      - metavariable-regex:
          metavariable: $USER_INPUT
          regex: ".*(request\.|user_|input_|query_|text_).*"

  - id: api-key-hardcoded
    message: Anthropic API key appears to be hardcoded. Use an environment variable.
    severity: ERROR
    languages: [python]
    patterns:
      - pattern: anthropic.Anthropic(api_key="sk-ant-...")
      - pattern: os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"] = "sk-ant-..."

  - id: subprocess-with-llm-output
    message: |
      subprocess called with a variable that may contain LLM-generated content.
      Validate and allowlist all command arguments before execution.
    severity: WARNING
    languages: [python]
    patterns:
      - pattern: subprocess.run($CMD, ...)
      - pattern: subprocess.call($CMD, ...)
    pattern-not:
      - pattern: subprocess.run(["..."], ...)  # only flag non-literal lists

  - id: llm-output-logged
    message: |
      Logging a full API response object may expose user data in log aggregators.
      Log only specific fields (usage, stop_reason) — not content.
    severity: WARNING
    languages: [python]
    pattern: |
      $LOG.info(..., $RESP, ...)
    metavariable-regex:
      metavariable: $RESP
      regex: ".*(response|resp|message|msg).*"

# Run custom rules against the codebase
semgrep --config security/semgrep-claude-rules.yaml app/ --json > /tmp/semgrep_report.json

# Also run the built-in security ruleset
semgrep --config p/python-security app/ --json >> /tmp/semgrep_report.json

# Check for secrets
detect-secrets scan --baseline .secrets.baseline app/
detect-secrets audit .secrets.baseline
Dependency Vulnerability Scanning

# Audit Python dependencies for known CVEs
pip-audit --requirement requirements.txt --output json > /tmp/pip_audit.json

# pip-audit checks PyPI Advisory Database (OSV).
# For anthropic SDK: always pin the version in requirements.txt
# and upgrade when security advisories are published.

# Alternative: use Safety (requires free API key)
pip install safety
safety check --full-report --output json > /tmp/safety_report.json
DAST with OWASP ZAP
DAST requires the application to be running. Run ZAP against a staging deployment, not production, so active scanning probes don't affect real users.

# Pull ZAP Docker image
docker pull ghcr.io/zaproxy/zaproxy:stable

# Baseline scan — passive, no active probing, fast (~2 min)
docker run --rm \
  -v $(pwd)/security:/zap/wrk/:rw \
  ghcr.io/zaproxy/zaproxy:stable \
  zap-baseline.py \
  -t https://staging.myapp.com \
  -J /zap/wrk/zap_baseline_report.json \
  -r /zap/wrk/zap_report.html \
  -l WARN

# Full active scan — may take 20-60 min
docker run --rm \
  -v $(pwd)/security:/zap/wrk/:rw \
  ghcr.io/zaproxy/zaproxy:stable \
  zap-full-scan.py \
  -t https://staging.myapp.com \
  -J /zap/wrk/zap_full_report.json \
  -x /zap/wrk/zap_report.xml
Custom ZAP Script for LLM Output Injection

# security/zap_llm_output_check.py # ZAP active scan extension: inject HTML/JS in API response, # check if it appears unescaped in the application's frontend. # Run via: zap-cli active-scan --script zap_llm_output_check.py import time INJECT_PAYLOADS = [ "", "", "javascript:alert(1)", ] def run_check(target_url: str, session) -> list[dict]: """ Simulate an LLM response containing XSS payloads and check if the frontend reflects them unsanitized. """ findings = [] for payload in INJECT_PAYLOADS: # Send a crafted request that causes the LLM mock to return the payload resp = session.post( f"{target_url}/api/chat", json={"message": f"Repeat this exactly: {payload}"}, ) if payload in resp.text: findings.append({ "type": "reflected_xss_from_llm_output", "payload": payload, "url": f"{target_url}/api/chat", "severity": "HIGH", "evidence": resp.text[:500], }) return findings
Parsing and Thresholding Security Findings
CI Workflow

# .github/workflows/security.yml
name: Security Scan

on:
  pull_request: {}
  push:
    branches: [main]
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 3 * * 1'   # weekly Monday 03:00 UTC

jobs:
  sast:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: '3.12'}
      - run: pip install bandit semgrep detect-secrets pip-audit

      - name: Bandit
        run: bandit -r app/ -ll -f json -o /tmp/bandit.json || true

      - name: Semgrep (custom + built-in)
        run: |
          semgrep --config security/semgrep-claude-rules.yaml app/ --json > /tmp/semgrep.json
          semgrep --config p/python-security app/ --json >> /tmp/semgrep.json

      - name: pip-audit
        run: pip-audit -r requirements.txt --output json > /tmp/pip_audit.json

      - name: Secret scanning
        run: detect-secrets scan app/ --all-files

      - name: Threshold check
        run: python security/parse_findings.py /tmp/bandit.json /tmp/semgrep.json

      - name: Upload findings
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: sast-findings
          path: /tmp/*.json

  dast:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'  # only on main, against staging
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: ZAP Baseline Scan
        uses: zaproxy/action-baseline@v0.14.0
        with:
          target: ${{ vars.STAGING_URL }}
          rules_file_name: security/zap_rules.tsv
          fail_action: true
          artifact_name: zap-baseline-report
False Positive Management
SAST tools generate false positives. Suppress them with inline comments rather than raising the threshold globally — suppressing globally hides real findings.

# Suppress a specific Bandit false positive inline:
result = subprocess.run(  # nosec B603 — args are validated against allowlist above
    ["convert", validated_input_path, output_path],
    capture_output=True,
    timeout=30,
)

# Suppress a Semgrep rule for a specific line:
logger.info("Request complete", extra={"stop_reason": resp.stop_reason})  # nosemgrep: llm-output-logged
# The suppression comment references the rule ID, not a generic disable.
Never use blanket disables like # noqa or # nosec without a rule ID. Rule-specific suppressions are reviewable; blanket disables are invisible security debt.
Summary

SAST for Claude applications requires both generic tools (Bandit, Semgrep built-in rules, pip-audit) and custom rules for LLM-specific risks — prompt injection from user input concatenation, API key hardcoding, subprocess calls with model-generated arguments, and LLM output logged without redaction. DAST with OWASP ZAP validates the running staging application for standard web vulnerabilities plus LLM output reflection. Block CI merges on HIGH and CRITICAL findings, suppress false positives with rule-specific inline comments, and run the full suite weekly in addition to every PR.
